title: XML Element

XML Element

XML Element

An XML element contains a start tag, content, and end tag.

<elementname> content </elementname>

Element with no contents: <elementname/>

  • content—any number of nested elements and/or literal text.

Conversion to Struct

An XML element converts to a Struct with the name elementname, and annotation tag xmlClass = element.

  • If the XML element contains a value, its corresponding Struct contains one scalar member with that value.
  • If the XML element contains other elements, its corresponding Struct contains members for each child element.
  • If the XML element contains a <![CDATA[...]]> construct, and the /full switch is used, the CDATA is converted to a separate nameless scalar Struct with the xmlClass tags set to CDATA. Otherwise, the CDATA is incorporated into one scalar Struct for the whole character value; it becomes indistinguishable from the rest of the data.
  • If /schema is specified, the element may have additional annotations.

Annotations

The Struct for an XML element can have the following annotations:

  • xmlClass = element
  • xmlNamespaceURI = URI
  • xmlNamespaceAlias = prefix

If xmlToStruct /schema is used, it can also have the following annotations:

  • xmlDataType = DataType
  • xmlTypeCategory = simple | complex
  • xmlTypeNamespace =

For more information, see Struct Annotations for XML.

Example: XML to Struct Conversion: Element with Data

An XML element containing character data:

<p>text</p>

String returned by $dbgString:

[]
 [p] = text
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element

You can get the values of Structs using Struct access operators. For example:

vValue = vStruct->p ; Result: vValue = "text"
vXmlType = vStruct->p->$tags->xmlClass ; Result: vXmlType = "element"

Example: XML to Struct Conversion: Element without Data

Empty elements can be designated using both open and closing tags or using the <element/> format. Both constructs are converted to an Struct. For example:

<div>
 <p></p>
 <p/>
</div>

When converted to a Struct, $dbgString returns:

[]
 [div]
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element
 [p] = ""
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element
 [p] = ""
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element

Example: XML to Struct Conversion: Element with Mixed Data

The following element contains both character data and another element.

<p><em>more</em>text</p>

In this case, the embedded element becomes a named Struct node, and the scalar member containing the character data (which is normally implicit), is made explicit:

[]
 [p]
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element
 [em] = more
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element
 text

For more information, see Struct Leaves.

Example: XML to Struct Conversion: <![CDATA[...]]>

The <![CDATA[...]]> construct is used to escape blocks of text containing characters which would otherwise be recognized as markup, and to keep whitespace and line breaks. For example, the following XML contains an XML message string embedded in the <p> element. Normally, this data would need to use entity substitutions < and > to prevent the message tags from being interpreted, but this makes the string difficult to read. By tagging it as <![CDATA[...]]>, it is treated as a normal string.

<p>Data: <![CDATA[<message from='Tim'>Please contact me.</message>]]></p>

When converted to a Struct, the CDATA becomes part of the value of the Struct.

[]
 [p] = Data: <message from='Tim'>Please contact me.</message>
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element

However, when converted to a Struct using xmlToStruct /full, the resulting Struct has two nameless members, and the CDATA member has an xmlClass annotation set to CDATA:

[]
 [p]
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = element
 Data: 
 <message from='Tim'>Please contact me.</message>
 [$tags]
 [xmlClass] = CDATA

This can affect expressions used to manipulate the data. For example, the following code shows the results of expressions that access the members of this Struct (referenced by vStruct). Notice that the CDATA scalar Struct has an xmlClass annotation set to CDATA, whereas the first scalar Struct has no annotation.

vValue = vStruct->p-> ; Result: vValue = "Data: <message from='Tim'>Please contact me.</message>"
vClass = vStruct->p->$tags->xmlClass ; Result: vClass = "element"
vClass = vStruct->p->*{1}->$tags->xmlClass ; Result: vClass = ""
vClass = vStruct->p->*{2}->$tags->xmlClass ; Result: vClass = "CDATA"
Was this topic helpful?
LikeDislike
